images.caption: Figure 1:LEAD framework.(1)SampleGGrollouts per promptqqfrom the old policyπθold\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}and score each by correctnessrcr_{c}.(2)Per-problem online target-length calibration: filter to correct rollouts𝒞q\mathcal{C}_{q}, setLq∗L^{*}_{q}to their mean, and compute the symmetric efficiency rewardrℓr_{\ell}, which peaks atℓ=Lq∗\ell{=}L^{*}_{q}and decays linearly to−1-1on either side.(3)Dynamic reward weighting: each reward is group-normalized separately to produce decoupled advantagesAq,j(c)A^{(c)}_{q,j}andAq,j(ℓ)A^{(\ell)}_{q,j}, and the PSI controller adapts the weights(λc(t),λℓ(t))(\lambda_{c}^{(t)},\lambda_{\ell}^{(t)})online from per-reward instability and headroom.(4)The two advantage channels are linearly combined under the EMA-smoothed weights and batch-whitened to obtain the effective advantageAq,jA_{q,j}used in the GRPO objective.Blue-framedcomponents are LEAD’s contributions;gray-framedcomponents are inherited from GRPO.

Figure 2:Training trajectories of the four aggregator variants on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B.(a)On-policy batch accuracy.(b)Mean response length on the rollout batch.(c)Per-problem targetLq∗L^{*}_{q}averaged over solvable prompts.(d)Symmetric efficiency rewardrℓr_{\ell}averaged over correct rollouts.

Figure 3:Training dynamics of a LEAD run on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B(4K budget,Bmax=4,000B_{\mathrm{max}}{=}4{,}000).(a)Dynamic weightsλc(t),λℓ(t)\lambda_{c}^{(t)},\lambda_{\ell}^{(t)}.(b)Per-promptLq∗L^{*}_{q}statistics across solvable prompts (mean and min–max range) and the count of unsolved prompts (assignedBmaxB_{\mathrm{max}}).(c)Rolling mean response length on the rollout batch and validation accuracy on MATH-500.

Figure 4:Per-prompt token allocation by base-difficulty tieron the pooled55-benchmark eval set (1,2751{,}275prompts). Difficulty is1−accbase​(q)1-\mathrm{acc}_{\mathrm{base}}(q)from the unmodified base model. Prompts are grouped into four tiers by base pass-rate;Q4Q_{4}collapses allacc=1\mathrm{acc}{=}1prompts (510510prompts) into one bin to avoid an arbitrary task-driven split of tied perfect-pass prompts.(a)Mean response length per tier, with Spearmanρ\rhobetween difficulty and length in the legend (higherρ\rho= greater difficulty-sensitivity). LEAD (ρ=+0.67\rho{=}+0.67) tracks the base model (ρ=+0.71\rho{=}+0.71) most closely; GDPO (ρ=+0.53\rho{=}+0.53) compresses most uniformly.(b)Average per-prompt extra tokens spent by LEAD over each baseline,Δ​ℓ​(q)=ℓLEAD​(q)−ℓB​(q)\Delta\ell(q)=\ell_{\mathrm{LEAD}}(q)-\ell_{B}(q), averaged within each tier. Against the strongest compression baselines (GDPO, ShorterBetter, GRPO), the gap on the hardest tier is2.22.2–3.4×3.4\timeslarger than on the easiest, showing that LEAD’s extra tokens go preferentially to harder prompts. Note: this panel aggregates per-prompt across the1,2751{,}275-prompt evaluation set (so MATH-500 and OlympiadBench, which are larger, dominate the overall mean), whereas Table1’s “Average Length” is averaged across the five benchmarks with equal weight; the two aggregations can disagree on which method is shorter overall when per-task mean lengths differ widely.
